coredata.dc:title: Hetero-shaped coral-like catalysts through metal-support interaction between nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots and PtPd alloy for oxygen reduction reaction
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Shanshan
Wang, Wei
Hu, Yanli
Tian, Feng
Miao, Xiaran
Liu, Liangsen
Xu, Zhiwei
coredata.dc:description: The development of efficient and durable electrocatalysts is essential for the practical application of fuel cells. Metal-support interaction (MSI) is an important concept in heterogeneous catalysis, which can construct the structure of catalyst and improve the reaction activity and stability of catalyst. Based on this, a simple and efficient method is innovatively proposed to synthesize coral-like special structure catalyst by utilizing the crush capacity of high energy γ-rays and the structure-induced effect of MSI between PtPd alloy and nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots. The electrocatalyst demonstrates superior long-term durability due to the structure of catalyst is not easy to collapse under suitable MSI. Meanwhile, the unique coral-like structure provides a larger contact area, abundant mass transfer channels, and full exposure of active sites, which are advantageous to improve electrocatalytic activity. The oxygen reduction reaction performance of optimal catalyst (NGQDs140-PtPd) was far superior to that of the commercial 20% Pt/C in alkaline medium, including onset potential (959 mV), half-wave potential (860 mV) and stability (the residual current density was 86.5% after long-term stability test). Therefore, coral-like shaped catalysts are expected to become a member of high-efficiency oxygen reduction catalysts in practical applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Hetero-shaped coral-like
Gamma irradiation
Metal-support interaction
Platinum–palladium alloy
Oxygen reduction reaction

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Electrocatalytic performance of all prepared samples d
Crystalline structures of all prepared samples
Structure and micromorphology of optimal samples
Original catalytic activity and durability of optimal samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) CV curves of NGQDs100-PtPd (curve a), NGQDs100-Pt1Pd2 (curve b), NGQDs100-Pt1Pd4 (curve c), NGQDs100-Pt1Pd6 (curve d), NGQDs100-Pt2Pd1 (curve e), NGQDs100-Pt4Pd1 (curve f), and NGQDs100-Pt6Pd1 (curve g). (b) LSV curves of different molar ratio metals catalysts at a speed of 1600 rpm; (c) CV curves of NGQDs60-PtPd (curve a), NGQDs100-PtPd (curve b), NGQDs140-PtPd (curve c), NGQDs180-PtPd (curve d); (d) LSV curves of different irradiation doses catalysts at a speed of 1600 rpm; (e) Tafel slopes of samples derived from Fig. 1(b) and (d); (f) The onset potential and the half-wave potential of NGQDs60-PtPd (curve a), NGQDs100-PtPd (curve b), NGQDs140-PtPd (curve c), NGQDs180-PtPd (curve d), NGQDs100-Pt1Pd2(curve e), NGQDs100-Pt1Pd4 (curve f), NGQDs100-Pt1Pd6 (curve g), NGQDs100-Pt2Pd1 (curve h), NGQDs100-Pt4Pd1 (curve i), NGQDs100-Pt6Pd1 (curve j) and Pt/C (curve k).

XRD patterns of Fig. (a) NGQDs100-PtPd, NGQDs100-Pt1Pd2, NGQDs100-Pt1Pd4, NGQDs100-Pt1Pd6, NGQDs100-Pt2Pd1, NGQDs100-Pt4Pd1, NGQDs100-Pt6Pd1; (b)NGQDs60-PtPd, NGQDs100-PtPd, NGQDs140-PtPd, NGQDs180-PtPd.

XPS spectra of experimental samples (a) N 1s XPS spectra of NGQDs. (b) Pt 4f spectra, and (c) the Pd 3d spectrum of NGQDs140-PtPd. (d) comparison of Pt 4f XPS spectra of NGQDs100-PtPd, NGQDs140-PtPd and Commercial Pt/C.

HRTEM images of (a) NGQDs140-PtPd, defects inside white wireframe. (Inset: real coral photos); (b) NGQDs140-PtPd (Inset: enlarged TEM image of the area showed in Fig. 3(b)); (c) and (d) Different locations of NGQDs100-PtPd; (e) and (f) Different locations of Pt/C; (g) the STEM-HAADF image and corresponding STEM-EDS elemental mapping of C, N, O Pt and Pd atoms for NGQDs140-PtPd.
(a) Emission fluorescence spectra of the prepared samples at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm; (b) FTIR spectra of as-prepared NGQDs and NGQDs140-PtPd.
Schematic illustration of preparation of NGQDs-PtPd.

(a) Nyquist plots of NGQDs100-PtPd, NGQDs140-PtPd and commercial Pt/C (The inset are the magnification of plots), in the frequency range of 105 Hz to 0.01 Hz; (b) LSV curves of NGQDs140-PtPd at different rotating speeds; (c) K-L curves of NGQDs140-PtPd at different potentials;.
(a) RRDE voltammograms of NGQDs140-PtPd and Pt/C catalyst at a rotation rate of 1600 rpm; (b) calculated by RRDE, n and %H2O2 yield of NGQDs140-PtPd and Pt/C at potential ranges over 0.2 to 1.1 V.
(a) Amperometric i-t curves of NGQDs140-PtPd and Pt/C in O2-saturated 0.1 M KOH solution at 0.5 V vs. RHE; (b) Initial and post-CA test LSV curves of NGQDs140-PtPd and Pt/C.
CV curves for NGQDs140-PtPd (a), and Pt/C (b) in Ar-saturated 0.1 M KOH electrolyte before and after ADT.
Concentration ratios of metal and irradiation doses of each system in obtained mixture.
Comparison of electrochemical results of prepared samples and commercial Pt/C.

head.ce:title: Hetero-shaped coral-like catalysts through metal-support interaction between nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots and PtPd alloy for oxygen reduction reaction

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Separation Membranes and Membrane Processes, School of Textile Science and Engineering, Tiangong University, Tianjin 300387, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Separation Membranes and Membrane Processes
School of Textile Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aState Key Laboratory of Separation Membranes and Membrane Processes, School of Textile Science and Engineering, Tiangong University, Tianjin 300387, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shanghai Synchrotron Radiation Facility, Zhangjiang Lab, Shanghai Advanced Research Institute, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Shanghai 201204, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bShanghai Synchrotron Radiation Facility, Zhangjiang Lab, Shanghai Advanced Research Institute, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Shanghai 201204, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The development of efficient and durable electrocatalysts is essential for the practical application of fuel cells. Metal-support interaction (MSI) is an important concept in heterogeneous catalysis, which can construct the structure of catalyst and improve the reaction activity and stability of catalyst. Based on this, a simple and efficient method is innovatively proposed to synthesize coral-like special structure catalyst by utilizing the crush capacity of high energy γ-rays and the structure-induced effect of MSI between PtPd alloy and nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots. The electrocatalyst demonstrates superior long-term durability due to the structure of catalyst is not easy to collapse under suitable MSI. Meanwhile, the unique coral-like structure provides a larger contact area, abundant mass transfer channels, and full exposure of active sites, which are advantageous to improve electrocatalytic activity. The oxygen reduction reaction performance of optimal catalyst (NGQDs140-PtPd) was far superior to that of the commercial 20% Pt/C in alkaline medium, including onset potential (959 mV), half-wave potential (860 mV) and stability (the residual current density was 86.5% after long-term stability test). Therefore, coral-like shaped catalysts are expected to become a member of high-efficiency oxygen reduction catalysts in practical applications.

ce:section.ce:para: With the advent of the energy crisis, people have gradually realized the importance of clean energy [1–3]. While fuel cells have become a new energy source that has attracted worldwide attention, it has great shortcomings in practical applications [4,5]. Catalyst must be used to promote the reaction due to the high overpotential and the sluggish kinetics of the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) at the cathode which are major obstacle hindering the development of fuel cells [6–9]. Platinum catalysts are generally thought to be the most prospective oxygen reduction reaction catalysts, but there are still problems such as high price, slow kinetics, and poor stability that hamper the development of catalysis [10–12]. Although many scientists focus on non-platinum catalysts, non-platinum catalysts cannot directly obtain the oxygen 4e−reduction process [13]. The increasing cost of Pt and the low abundance triggered scientists to develop Pt-based bimetallic catalysts for fuel cell applications.

One of the most interesting aspects of catalytic research is the synergy and/or synergy of bimetallic catalysts [14]. The introduction of a second metal (M¼ Pd, Fe, Ni, Co, etc.) which can improve the catalytic performance via ligand and strain effects by altering the binding strength of Pt-oxygen intermediates [8,15–17]. In recent years, Platinum–palladium (PtPd) alloy is regarded as one of the candidate materials with research potential, which can not only have better catalytic performance than pure Pt catalyst, but also reduce the cost of catalyst production due to abundant palladium reserves and low prices [18,19]. Therefore, PtPd bimetal catalysis is gradually recognized and further studied by more and more people.

The surface properties of the catalyst will change with the carrier, thus it is concluded that the support determines the performance of the catalyst to a certain extent [20–23]. One of the most important research topics in heterogeneous catalysis is metal-support interaction (MSI) [24,25]. This interaction force is mainly reflected in the regulation of the electronic structure and geometric structure of dispersed metals [25]. Meanwhile, the interaction between the metal and the support is also considered to play a key role in regulating selectivity, stability, and the activity of the catalyst [26,27]. Previous research results indicate that the carrier usually involved in the strong metal-support interaction effect is a reducible metal oxide (TiO2, CeO2, etc.) [28]. However, by reason of its large specific surface area and good electrical conductivity, carbon materials are often used as a carrier for electrocatalytic catalyst [29–31]. Recently, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have received considerable attention due to their advantageous characteristics, and have become potential oxygen reduction catalyst materials owing to high electrical conductivity, rich surface functional groups, the fast electron transfer via pep conjugated network, especially small size with rich edges (the edge carbon atoms have better catalytic activity than carbon atoms in the base plane.) [32–35]. In addition, heteroatom doping of GQDs can adjust the intrinsic properties of such nanomaterials, especially nitrogen doping is more conducive to improving the electrocatalytic properties of ORR. For example, nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) have more defects and rich highly reactive oxygen-containing functional groups than GQDs which are beneficial to the improvement of oxygen reduction activity and the formation of composite materials [36–38].

In addition, preparation of noble metal-based nanocatalysts is generally not eco-friendly since it requires some poisonous reductants such as sodium borohydride and polyvinylpyrrolidone. However, based on the in-depth research on gamma irradiation [39–42], we discovered that gamma irradiation can not only induce reducing free radicals in aqueous solution systems to promote metal ion reduction, but also the fragmentation effect due to high energy is beneficial to the catalyst cutting of surface defects to form a unique catalyst structure [43]. Gamma irradiation has proven to be a powerful tool for the preparation of highly dispersed precious metal nanoparticles, which without the need to add chemical reducing agents throughout the preparation process.

Herein, we have designed an alkaline ORR catalyst with high activity and stability. By adjusting the radiation dose and the metal ratio, the MSI between the NGQDs rich in functional groups on the surface and the PtPd alloy is optimized. We utilize the synergy of MSI induced construction of the catalyst structure and the unique fragmentation ability of high-energy gamma rays to prevent excessive catalyst growth, resulting in the formation of a special coral-like structure. Under the effect of MSI between the NGQDs and PtPd alloy, the coral-like structure is more stable and not easy to collapse, as well as the unique structure gives abundant mass transfer channels and full exposure of active sites, which will make the catalyst more efficient and durable. In addition, the ligand effect and crystal strain generated between the platinum and palladium alloys will also bring a downshift of d-band center, thereby weakening of the adsorption energy of oxygen atoms, and lowering the energy barrier of the oxygen reduction reaction.

Commercial 20% Pt/C was purchased from Johnson Matthey Company. Nafion (5 wt%) from DuPont. Chloroplatinic acid hexahydrate (H2PtCl6•6H2O, 98%) was obtained from Tianjin Yingda Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. Urea (99%) was obtained from Tianjin Kemiou Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd, palladium chloride (PdCl2, 99%) and BrijⓇ L4 polyoxyethylene laurel ether (Brij30Ⓡ) were bought from aladdin Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd, Citrc acid, hexane (C6H14, 95%) and isopropanol (C3H8O, 99%) were bought from Tianjin Yongda Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. All the reagents used are analytical reagents, which need not be purified again before use.

NGQDs were synthesized by a general hydrothermal method [34]. First, 5 mmol of citric acid and 5 mmol of urea were added to 20 ml of ultrapure water and transferred to a 50 ml Teflflon-lined autoclave after stirred uniformly. Then, the sealed autoclave was heated at 180 °C for 6 h. Finally, it was stood until room temperature, and the obtained solution was dialyzed for 2 d. The remaining solution was freeze-dried to obtain a blue-black solid, i.e. the required purified NGQDs.

In this work, platinum–palladium alloy/nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs-PtPd) with different metal molar ratios, as shown in Table 1 , including samples NGQDs100-PtPd, NGQDs100-Pt1Pd2, NGQDs100-Pt1Pd4, NGQDs100-Pt1Pd6, NGQDs100-Pt2Pd1, NGQDs100-Pt4Pd1 and NGQDs100-Pt6Pd1 which were prepared with Pt:Pd atomic ratio1:1, 1:2, 1:4, 1:6, 2:1, 4:1, 6:1 under the gamma irradiation dose of 100 kGy, respectively. Typical sample NGQDs100-PtPd was synthesized under the synergetic effect of gamma irradiation and microemulsion. The details of the experiment were as follows [44]: 100 mL microemulsion solution was produced in Brij30Ⓡ/hexane/isopropanol at a ratio of 9.5:54.5:36.5% v/v and dispersed in NGQDs solution (66 mL, 6 mg/mL). After that, H2PtCl6•6H2O (5.63 mL, 0.05 mM) and PdCl2 (5.63 mL, 0.05 mM) were dispersed into the mixture. Ultrapure water was added to the prepared solution until it reached 200 ml, and the solution was then vigorously stirred for 24 h to achieve ion exchange. Finally, the mixture was purged in an inert N2 atmosphere for 30 min to remove the air. After sealing and then exposed to the 60Co gamma irradiation source with absorbed doses of 100 kGy (at a dose rate of 2.7 kGy/h). After irradiation, the prepared NGQDs-PtPd nanocomposite suspensions appear milky white with a mixture of black flaky particles. Subsequently, with 10,000 rpm centrifugation purify NGQDs-PtPd composite suspension 5 min, reoccupy ultrapure water washing for many times, eliminate Pt4+, Pd3+ and surfactant molecules. And then in the internal pressure is less than 10 Pa, freeze drying of condensation temperature of 223.15 K. After lyophilization, eventually get NGQDs100-PtPd powder. For the preparation of NGQDs100-Pt1Pd2, NGQDs100-Pt1Pd4, NGQDs100-Pt1Pd6, NGQDs100-Pt2Pd1, NGQDs100-Pt4Pd1 and NGQDs100-Pt6Pd1 in this experiment, change the atomic ratio only if other conditions are constant.

Further, we conducted the following experiments in order to investigations the effect of irradiation dose. NGQDs-PtPd at different irradiation doses contains samples NGQDs60-PtPd, NGQDs100-PtPd, NGQDs140-PtPd and NGQDs180-PtPd, respectively, which were obtained by exposed to the 60Co gamma irradiation source with absorbed doses of 60 kGy, 100 kGy, 140 kGy and 180 kGy (Table 1). The experimental operation is consistent with NGQDs100-PtPd and the molar ratio of metal is 1:1, which only changes the gamma irradiation dose.

The crystalline structures of NGQDs-PtPd were investigate by X-ray diffraction making use of the Bruker D8 Discover XRD with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.540 59 Å) at a scanning speed of 10°/min from 20° to 90° Using the JEM-2100 model instrument at 300 kV, the HRTEM image of the experimental sample was taken to observe its morphology and nanostructure. Analyze the structure of the prepared sample using Fourier Transform Infrared Spectrometer (Nicolet iS50). The fluorescence performance of the sample was observed using a steady-state transient fluorescence spectrometer (FLS1000). The thermogravimetric analyzer (TG 209 F3 Tarsus) was used to detect sample composition at the temperature range from 25 °C (room temperature) to 800 °C under the airflow with a ramp of 10 °C min−1 to obtain the thermogravimetric (TG) curve.

Electrochemical measurements were carried out at CHI760E electrochemical station. The reference electrode and counter electrode were Ag/AgCl electrode and Pt foil electrode respectively. The preparation method of the working electrode is as follows: 3 mg of the dried catalyst was weighed and dispersed in 10 μL 5 wt% Nafion and 990 μL ethanol solution, sonicated until the catalyst is completely dispersed in ethanol, at least 60 min, and then 12 μL of the suspension was moved to the Φ5 mm glassy carbon disk and dried at room temperature. Durability were evaluated by chronoamperometry (CA) and the accelerated degradation test (ADT). The CV curves were recorded at a scanning rate of 50 mV•s−1 in Ar-saturated 0.1 M KOH solution, and the polarization curve of ORR was measured in an O2-saturated 0.1 M KOH solution at 1600 rpm with a scanning rate of 10 mV•s−1. The 20 wt% Commercial Pt/C catalyst was also investigated for comparison. The relationship between all potentials and RHE was reported by converting the measured potentials using the following formula:
(1)
E R H E = E A g / A g C l + 0.059 × p H + 0.197

The tafel plot is used to evaluate the difficulty of the kinetic reaction during the ORR process. The E-logjk curve is made according to the polarization curve, and the slope of the curve is calculated. Ik represents the kinetic current density, which is obtained by in the following equation.
(2)

Calculation of the number of transferred electrons according to the Koutecky–Levich (K–L) equation (if the calculated value is slightly greater than 4, this is normal.):

In the calculation process, i represents the test current, ik represents the kinetic current, and the limit diffusion current is represented by id. ω is the electrode speed, B is the slope of the curve, 96,485 C•mol−1, 1.9 × 10−5 cm2•s − 1, 0.01 cm2•s − 1, and 1.2 × 10−6 mol∙cm−3 are Faraday constants (F), diffusion coefficient (D), kinetic viscosity (υ), and concentration of O2 (C). The tafel curve is used to evaluate the difficulty of the dynamic response in the ORR process. The E-logjk curve is made according to the polarization curve, and the slope of the curve is calculated. Ik represents the kinetic current density, which is obtained by Eq. (2).

Rotating ring disk electrode (RRDE) calculation of hydrogen peroxide yield and number of transferred electrons n:
(5)

I r

In the formula calculation, n represents the number of electrons transferred in the ORR process,%H2O2 represents the yield of H2O2 in solution, the collection coefficient is N (0.37), the ring current is Ir and the disk current is represented by Id .
The electrochemical impedance spectrum (EIS) was investigated in a frequency range of 106 to 1 Hz with an AC voltage of 5 mV in 0.1 M KOH solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrocatalytic performance of all prepared samples d
ce:section.ce:para: The electrocatalytic activity of the as-prepared catalyst was first tested by cyclic voltammetry (CV) with a sweep speed of 50 mV•s−1 in Ar-saturated 0.1 M KOH solution (Fig. 1 ). As displayed in Fig. 1(a) and (c), the comparison results about the CV characteristics of NGQDs-PtPd reveals that their ORR activities are greatly influenced by the Pt:Pd ratio and radiation doses. The catalysts performance with different Pt:Pd ratio are shown in Fig. 1(a). When the Pt:Pd ratio is 1:2, 1:4, 1:6, 2:1, 4:1 and 6:1, the peak potentials have a certain degree of negative shift compared with the samples of 1:1 . The sample NGQDs100-PtPd with Pt:Pd ratio of 1: 1 has more positive peak potential compared with samples of the other Pt:Pd ratios at the 100 kGy irradiation doses, which indicates that more active sites of catalysts are involved in the ORR process, and thus is beneficial to the occurrence of ORR. The change of Pt:Pd ratio can affect the ligand effect between alloys. We guess that the downshift of the center of the d-band caused by the ligand effect is the most benefitial to the ORR when the Pt:Pd ratio is 1:1. Compared with other radiation doses, the peak potential of sample NGQDs140-PtPd is the most positive, indicating that the catalyst exposes more catalytic active sites when the radiation dose is 140 kGy (Fig. 1(c)).

The ORR performance was further study by rotating disk electrodes (RDE) measurements. At a rotating rate of 1600 rpm, the linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) was measured with RDE to further study the oxygen reduction performance of the catalyst. Fig. 1(b) displays the polarization curves of different Pt:Pd molar ratios, and the ORR performance first increases obviously as the Pt:Pd molar ratios decreases from 6:1 to 1:1. However, a further decrement of Pt:Pd is inhibit the ORR process, resulting in a significant decay of current density. When evaluating oxygen reduction activity, the adsorption energy of oxygen atoms is a commonly used evaluation index. The optimal value of adsorption energy of oxygen atoms is 0.2 eV weaker than the one for Pt (111). According to the experimental results and previous studies, it is speculated that platinum produces corresponding surface lattice strain and coordination environment changes due to the addition of metal palladium, which in turn causes the center of the d-band downshift and the adsorption energy of the oxygen atom decreases closest to ~0.2 eV. And Fig. 1(d) displays the polarization curves of the catalysts with different irradiation doses, and with the increase of radiation doses, the ORR performance of the catalysts shows a trend of increasing first and then decreasing, and NGQDs140-PtPd exists an optimal ORR performance among samples with the different radiation doses. We speculate that the radiation dose is less than 140 kGy, the weaker energy leads to poor reduction and crushing of the metal. If the radiation dose is exceeding 140 kGy, the structure of the catalyst will be destroyed. These figures illustrate that the performance of the catalyst is better and agrees well with the results of CV performance. The onset potential, half-wave potential, and limited diffusion current density of all catalysts are shown in Table 2 and Fig. 1(f). From Table 2 and Fig. 1(f), it can be seen that the catalytic activity of NGQDs140-PtPd is optimal while the irradiation dose is 140 kGy and the Pt: Pd molar ratio is 1:1, the initial potential reaches 959 mV, which is 96 mV higher than commercial Pt/C, and the half-wave potential is 870 mV, which is higher than commercial Pt/C 95 mV higher, limit diffusion current reached 5.658 mA•cm−2. In addition, compared with other current alloy catalysts (Table S1), the catalytic performance of NGQDs140-PtPd is still very superior. Overall, the obtained catalyst with the Pt:Pd ratio of 1:1 under the irradiation dose of 140 kGy is most beneficial to improve the electrochemical performance.

The tafel slope is related to the overpotential of the catalyst, which can be used to research ORR kinetics and further test the catalytic performance of the catalyst. According to the LSV curve of the tested sample, the tafel diagram in Fig. 1(e) can be calculated by Eq. (2). According to Fig. 1(e) and Table 2, compared with other samples, NGQDs140-PtPd (77 mV•dec−1) has a smaller tafel slope, suggesting his overpotential is also much smaller than that of the samples under the other Pt:Pd ratio and irradiation dose, and further demonstrating its more rapid kinetics for the ORR, which can be attribute to the large specific surface area and the exposure of more catalytically active sites brought about by its irregular coral-like morphology.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Crystalline structures of all prepared samples
ce:section.ce:para: The XRD pattern in Fig. 2 showed that NGQDs successfully combined with PtPd alloy. In the XRD patterns of NGQDs140-PtPd, the four diffraction peaks appeared at 39.9°, 46.21°, 67.7° and 81.8° are ascribed to the (111), (200), (220) and (311) diffraction faces of the fcc Pt–Pd alloy, respectively. All four diffraction peaks can be observed in the XRD pattern of the NGQDS-PtPd catalyst and the position of these diffraction peaks is between single Pt (JCPDS-04–0802 Pt) and Pd (JCPDS-46–1043 Pd), which means the formation of PtPd alloy structures. In addition, the NGQDs XRD pattern in Fig. 2 showed a broad diffraction peak of C (002) about 23°, indicating that the formation of NGQDs and PtPd alloy composites.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structure and micromorphology of optimal samples
ce:section.ce:para: XPS was conducted to investigate the chemical state of sample surface. It can be observed that NGQDs100-PtPd and NGQDs140-PtPd possess the optimal ORR performance among different irradiation doses and metal ratio samples, respectively, as well as the performance of NGQDs140-PtPd is better than those of NGQDs100-PtPd. Thus, this work studied the structural differences between the two samples and focused on the structural characteristics of NGQDs140-PtPd.

Further study on chemical state of sample surface using XPS. Fig. 3 (a) illustrates the nitrogen element in the previously prepared NGQDs exists in three states, pyridine N (398.28 eV), pyrrolic N (398.98 eV) and graphite nitrogen (400.38 eV). In recent study, pyridinic N is considered as the most favorable forms of these nitrogen species presence for the ORR reaction. The pyridine N content in the sample accounts for more than 30%, which is conducive to improving the activity of the catalyst. The Pt and Pd chemical states are analyzed by XPS spectrum of NGQDs140-PtPd. The degree of reduction and valence of Pt and Pd can be seen from Pt 4f and Pd 3d in sample NGQDs140-PtPd. It is evident from Fig. 3(b) that the Pt 4f peak consists of two double lines: primary component Pt0 and secondary component Pt2+. The peaks appeared at 71.48 and 74.72 eV belong to metal Pt0 states. The peaks appeared at 72.31 and 75.94 eV are attributed to Pt2+ [45]. Similar to platinum, the 3d peak of palladium also splits into two peaks in Fig. 3(c). The peak positions at 335.6 eV (Pd 3d5/2) and 341.5 eV (Pd 3d3/2) belonged to Pd0 [46], another doublet at 336.4 eV (Pd 3d5/2) and 341.6 eV (Pd 3d3/2) belonged to Pd2+. It can be seen from the Fig. 4 (b) and (c) that under the reduction effect of gamma irradiation, Pt and Pd mainly exist in an atomic state, which is beneficial for the enrichment of catalytic activity. As shown in Fig. S1(a), compared with NGQDs100-Pt and Pt/C, the Pt peak obviously moves to the direction of low binding energy, likely because of significant shift is due to MSI result in the transfer of electrons from NGQDs to Pt nanoparticles, then, increasing the electron cloud density around Pt atoms. And NGQDs100-PtPd further moved to the direction of low binding energy compared to NGQDs100-Pt, which may be due to the electronic donation of Pt to Pd (Fig. S1(b)). From the peak position (Fig. 3(d)), compare to NGQDs100-PtPd and Pt/C, the Pt 4f of NGQDs140-PtPd is shifted 0.3 eV, 0.8 eV, respectively. Besides, the Pd 3d peak shifts to a higher value compared to the standard palladium peak. Due to the ligand effect, the downshift of the center of the d-band will affect the adsorption of oxygen intermediates between there active catalyst surface and adsorbed OH species (OHad) and essentially lower the Pt/Pd-OHad bond energy [47].

The morphology and structure of NGQDs-PtPd under different alloy proportions and irradiation doses were observed by HRTEM (Fig. 4 and Fig. S2). We can obviously see that the prepared NGQDs140-PtPd has a morphology similar to the upper right corner coral which is branched and increases the direct contact area of the catalyst, and there are more atomic defects on the catalyst surface, such as the area selected by the white wireframe (Fig. 4(a)). The unique hetero-shaped coral-like can increase the specific surface area, enrich surface and corner defects, expose more active sites, which is favorable to the ORR performance. We guess that a large radiation dose is conducive to the formation of defects. Fig. 4(b) shows that the dendritic morphology of the catalyst is obvious, and the metal lattice fringe is 0.245 nm, which is between Pt (0.2650 nm) and Pd (0.2246 nm), which indicates that the alloy structure is formed. Compared with NGQDs140-PtPd, NGQDs100-PtPd does not form a branch structure that extends outward, and has fewer atomic defects on the surface, which directly leads to a slightly worse oxygen reduction performance of NGQDs140-PtPd (Fig. 4(c) and (d)). In addition, commercial Pt/C catalyst has a regular spherical structure and is slightly agglomerated (Fig. 4(e) and (f)). According to STEM-HAADF image and corresponding STEM-EDS elemental mapping (Fig. 4(g)), the NGQDs140-PtPd catalyst is rich in nitrogen and oxygen elements, which is consistent with the previous XPS data. Moreover, the loaded metals Pt and Pd are evenly distributed on the NGQDs140-PtPd catalyst. And according to the TG test results (Fig. S5), the NGQDs140-PtPd catalyst is composed of 23 wt% Pt and Pd, which shows that the metal loading of the completely reduced final products is almost the same as that used in the synthesis mixture.

Electron transfer between NGQDs and platinum palladium alloy was found from XPS data, which proved their interaction. For a deeper understanding, the optimal sample was characterized by steady-state transient fluorescence spectrometer and FTIR. From Fig. 5 (a) and Fig. S3, it can be seen that the fluorescence was obviously weakened after gamma irradiation. The surface of our previously prepared NGQDs contains abundant functional groups (Fig. 5(b)). The two peaks at 3202 cm−1 and 3430 cm−1 are the stretching vibration [48] absorption peaks of -OH and -NH2, and the two absorption peaks at 1568 cm−1 and 1403 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibration of CO/CC and the bending vibration absorption characteristic band of CN, respectively; the stretching vibration peaks of CO single bond are also seen at 1110 cm−1; the two absorption peaks at 1110 cm−1 and 769 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibration and bending vibration absorption characteristic bands of CN, respectively. In addition, the absorption peak at 612 cm−1 is the swing vibration absorption peak of -NH2. Therefore, the appearance of these characteristic peaks indicates that the surface of the NGQDs is rich in carboxyl and amino functional groups. However, after irradiation, the peak intensity of the coral-shaped profiled NGQDs140-PtPd catalyst became significantly smaller, and some of the peaks disappeared. This also explains why the catalyst fluorescence decreases after irradiation. Due to electrostatic adsorption, Pt4+ and Pd2+ are adsorbed on the surface defects and negatively charged functional groups of NGQDs during the synthesis process. When metal ions were irradiated by γ-rays, they grew and nucleated on the surface of NGQDs to form alloys, and were firmly fixed by the remaining functional groups and abundant defects on the surface of NGQDs. The redistribution of functional groups on the surface of NGQDs promotes the interaction and binding between NGQDs and PtPd alloy. These results further demonstrate the existence of MSI between NGQDs and PtPd alloy, and that MSI can be regulated by controlling the alloy ratio and irradiation dose.

Coral-like shaped catalyst formation requires a suitable metal ratio and irradiation dose to change MSI between NGQDs and the alloy. As shown in Fig. 6 , the image of MSI can be likened to the mutual attraction between magnet and iron due to magnetic force to facilitate understanding, but MSI is the interaction generated by the electron transfer between the metal and the carrier. Due to the different forces between NGQDs and each alloy molecule, a coral-like irregular structure is formed under the action of MSI. The growth mechanism was plotted in Fig. 6:
n
H 2
O
( radiolysis )
→ n
e
−
aq
,
H
·
,
O
H
−
,
H
+
, … etc
.
P
t
4 +
+ 4
e
−
aq
→ P
t
0
P
d
2 +
+ 2
e
−
aq
→ P
d
0

Pt4+and Pd2+ is reduced to Pt0 and Pd0 by e − aq and nucleated on the surface of NGQDs, and NGQDs acted as a binder between different PtPd alloy nanoparticles. Then, the PtPd alloy and NGQDs freely combined to form a hetero-shaped coral-like catalyst under the induction of MSI. The surface of NGQDs is rich in oxygen-containing functional groups, which can fix metal nanoparticles well. In addition, the microemulsion used in the reaction can inhibit the agglomeration of nanoparticles and the excessive growth of coral-like catalysts. Some functional groups on the surface of NGQDs may be relocated during the formation of the complex due to their poor stability and the fluorescence is weakened after irradiation, which also proves the MSI between the metal and the support. This further proves that there is indeed an electron transfer between NGQDs and PtPd alloy, and that MSI really exists between NGQDs and PtPd alloy. The strong binding force is one of the reasons for the enhanced durability of the catalyst which the alloy with the main catalytic action is not easy to fall off in the base and the original catalyst morphology is easier to maintain. Such as, NGQDs140-PtPd after CA measurements with 20000s, the catalyst morphology is basically unchanged, and the branch structure at the edge is obvious (Fig. S4).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Original catalytic activity and durability of optimal samples
ce:section.ce:para: To further evaluate the catalytic activity, ECSA was calculated from the CV curves measured in Ar-saturated 0.5 M H2SO4 solution (Fig. S6). ECSA of NGQDs140-PtPd was 78.6 m2/gPt, which is substantially higher than that of NGQDs100-PtPd (75.3 m2/gPt) and Pt/C (65.6 m2/gPt) (Fig. S7). This indicates that the hetero-shaped coral-like catalysts formed by MSI fundamentally improves the catalytic activity, which is consistent with the electrochemical performance results. The charge transfer resistance and mass diffusion process of the catalyst were then evaluated according to EIS (Fig. 7 (a)). The slope of the inclined line of the best prepared catalyst is not significantly different from that the commercial Pt/C at low frequency, but the semicircle at the middle and high frequency reflects that the charge transfer resistance of NGQDs140-PtPd is much smaller than that of the commercial Pt/C, which further proves that the heterocaloric catalyst prepared has an advantage in improving ORR performance.

We further tested the polarization curves of NGQDs140-PtPd at different speeds (400 r/min, 625 r/min, 900 r/min, 1600 r/min) by RDE measurements in Fig. 7(b). The limit diffusion current increases with the rotation speed of the disk electrode. At the same time, using the K-L equation Eq. (3) and ((4)), the number of transferred electrons (n) of the catalyst can be calculated according to the planning curve at different speeds to study the kinetics of ORR. As can be seen from the K-L polt of catalysts at different potential in Fig. 7(c), the linear relationship of K-L diagrams of NGQDs140-PtPd indicates that the concentration of dissolved oxygen can be determined as the first-order reaction kinetics. The corresponding K-L curve shows a clear linear relationship in the potential range of 0.3 to 0.6 v. After calculation, NGQDs140-PtPd shows a complete 4e− (0.3 v, n = 4.1; 0.4 v, n = 3.95; 0.5 v, n = 3.94; 0.6 v, n = 4.12) reaction process and one step reduction from O2 to OH−. We also calculate the transfer electron number of the catalyst as well as the hydrogen peroxide yield (H2O2 is generated only during the 2e− reaction, and the content of H2O2 in the electrolyte solution can indirectly show the electron transfer path in the catalyst because H2O2 is generated only during the 2e− reaction.) by the method of RRDE, because the empirical coefficients in the K-L equation such as dissolved oxygen, diffusion coefficient, viscosity coefficient and so on, the actual system and the reference value of the literature may be different, resulting in certain errors in the calculation. The RRDE voltammograms of NGQDs140-PtPd and Pt/C are shown in Fig. 8 (a), both of which have a low ring current (Ir ) derived from HO2 − oxidation; Moreover, Fig. 8(b) shows the electron transfer number and%H2O2 yield of these samples that determined by Eq. (5) and (6). As can be seen from Fig. 8(b) and Table 2, the number of transferred electrons in NGQDs140-PtPd is 3.91, and the yield of H2O2 is only 4.75%, while the number of transferred electrons in commercial Pt/C is 3.51, and the yield of H2O2 is as high as 24.4%, which is about 5 times that of H2O2 in NGQDs140-PtPd. It is proved that the hetero-shaped coral-like catalyst has an excellent ORR activity and is a promising catalytic.

The service life of electrocatalysts in practical applications is especially determined by their durability. In other words, durability determines whether the catalysts can go from the laboratory to the mass production and industrialization. The electrochemical stability of NGQDs140-PtPd and commercial Pt/C was studied by CA test (Fig. 9 (a)). After testing for 37,000 s, NGQDs140-PtPd attenuates current density by less than 15%, while commercial Pt/C attenuates current density by about 35%. Further, the durability test was performed by LSV measurement, Fig. 9(b) displays that NGQDs140-PtPd gives a negligible activity loss after 37,000 s, smaller than that of Pt/C (38 and 42 mV, respectively), which indicates which indicates the stability of the optimal catalyst prepared is far exceeds the commercial Pt/C. We also measured the durability by ADT, the results show that the CV curves of the commercial Pt/C have a significantly change after 3000 cycles (Fig. 10 (b)). However, the NGQDs140-PtPd shows little change in CV curves even after 3000 cycles durability tests (Fig. 10(a), which is consistent with the results of the CA measurement. The rich functional groups on the surface of NGQDs can rely on MSI to firmly fix the metal nanoparticles, which is conducive to maintaining the morphology of the catalyst, thereby maintaining good cycle stability.

In summary, we prepared coral-like catalysts with special morphologies using NGQDs and PtPd alloy, based on MSI by adjusting gamma irradiation and metal proportions. This process is no use of any morphology inducer, inexpensive and green environmental protection. The main conclusions are as below:
(1)
NGQDs have rich marginal functional groups, which can serve as a carrier and a binder during the preparation process, which is beneficial to form a shaped catalyst.
(2)
Doping the second metal palladium to form PtPd alloy, due to lattice strain and coordination effects, reduces the adsorption energy of oxygen atoms, improves kinetic reaction speed and reduces costs.
(3)
Relying on MSI, on the one hand, coral-like catalyst stabilizes the morphology of the catalyst and improves the cycle stability. On the other hand, the electron transfer between the carrier and the metal, and the density of the outermost electron cloud of the metal increases, which is beneficial to improve the stability of catalyst.
(4)
By adjusting the radiation dose and metal ratio, NGQDs and PtPd alloy can form coral-like catalyst under irradiation and MSI induction which can increase the specific surface area, provide more surface and corner defects and expose more active sites.
(5)
The prepared NGQDs140-PtPd catalyst far exceeds the commercial Pt/C which has a high initial potential of 959 mV, a half-wave potential of 870 mV and the limiting current density decays less than 15% after a durability test of 37,000 s.

Shanshan Liu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Investigation, Writing - original draft. Wei Wang: Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Visualization, Software. Yanli Hu: Validation, Formal analysis, Visualization. Feng Tian: Writing - original draft, Software. Xiaran Miao: Writing - original draft, Software. Liangsen Liu: Resources, Supervision, Data curation. Zhiwei Xu: Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work was funded by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (11575126, U2032133).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.electacta.2020.137314.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Understanding composition-dependent synergy of PtPd alloy nanoparticles in electrocatalytic oxygen reduction reaction

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Pd/TiO nanocatalyst with strong metal-support interaction for highly efficient durable heterogeneous hydrogenation

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Imran, M.; Yousaf, A.B.; Zhou, X.; Jiang, Y.-.F.; Yuan, C.-.Z.; Zeb, A.; Jiang, N.; Xu, A.-.W., Pd/TiO nanocatalyst with strong metal-support interaction for highly efficient durable heterogeneous hydrogenation. J. Phys. Chem. C2017, 121, 1162–1170.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Engineering bunched Pt–Ni alloy nanocages for efficient oxygen reduction in practical fuel cells

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X Tian, X. Z., Ya-Q Su, L Wang, H Wang, Engineering bunched Pt–Ni alloy nanocages for efficient oxygen reduction in practical fuel cells. Sci. Sports2019, 366 (6467), 850–856.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ultra-thin double janus nanofiltration membrane for separation of Li+ and Mg2+: “Drag” effect from carboxyl-containing negative interlayer

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Guo, C.; Li, N.; Qian, X.; Shi, J.; Jing, M.; Teng, K.; Xu, Z., Ultra-thin double janus nanofiltration membrane for separation of Li+ and Mg2+: “Drag” effect from carboxyl-containing negative interlayer. Sep. Purif. Technol.2020, 230, 115567.
